lessonTitle: "Remember This Day, in Which Ye Came Out from Egypt": Exodus 7–13
quickInsight: A hard heart keeps you stuck. A soft one lets God set you free.

quotableVerses.text: I, Nephi, being exceedingly young, nevertheless being large in stature, and also having great desires to know of the mysteries of God, wherefore, I did cry unto the Lord; and behold he did visit me, and did soften my heart that I did believe all the words which had been spoken by my father; wherefore, I did not rebel against him like unto my brothers.

And the blood shall be to you for a token upon the houses where ye are: and when I see the blood, I will pass over you, and the plague shall not be upon you to destroy you, when I smite the land of Egypt.
The next day John seeth Jesus coming unto him, and saith, Behold the Lamb of God, which taketh away the sin of the world.

And now, my sons, remember, remember that it is upon the rock of our Redeemer, who is Christ, the Son of God, that ye must build your foundation; that when the devil shall send forth his mighty winds, yea, his shafts in the whirlwind, yea, when all his hail and his mighty storm shall beat upon you, it shall have no power over you to drag you down to the gulf of misery and endless wo, because of the rock upon which ye are built, which is a sure foundation, a foundation whereon if men build they cannot fall.

sections.title: The Story
items: Pharaoh enslaved Israel and refused to let them go — so God sent ten plagues to show His power and force Pharaoh's hand.
Each time a plague hit, Pharaoh would panic and promise to let Israel go — then as soon as things calmed down, he'd harden his heart and go back on his word.
The tenth plague was the death of the firstborn — but God gave Israel a way out: sacrifice a perfect lamb, put its blood on the doorpost, and the destroyer would pass over their homes.
Pharaoh's firstborn died. He finally let Israel go. God told them to remember this day forever — because He delivered them from slavery.

sections.title: Hard Heart vs. Soft Heart
items: Pharaoh's pattern is scarily relatable: feel the pressure, make a promise, then go right back to the same choices once things cool off. Ever done that after a testimony meeting or camp? Felt something real, promised to change, then slid back by Tuesday?
A hard heart isn't always rebellion — sometimes it's just stubbornness. Refusing to apologize. Ignoring a prompting. Choosing your ego over what you know is right.
Nephi was young like you when he 'did cry unto the Lord; and behold he did visit me, and did soften my heart' (1 Nephi 2:16). He didn't have it figured out — he just asked. That's the difference.
The manual compares a hard heart to a rock and a soft heart to a sponge. One lets water bounce off, the other absorbs it. When the Spirit speaks in seminary or sacrament meeting, which one are you?

sections.title: The Passover Points to Jesus Christ
items: The lamb had to be perfect, 'without blemish' — just like the Savior lived a sinless life so He could be our sacrifice (see 1 Peter 1:19).
The blood on the doorpost saved Israel from destruction. We're saved the same way — through the blood of Christ and our faith in His Atonement (see Mosiah 4:2).
Unleavened bread = no corruption. Bitter herbs = the bitterness of captivity and sin. Eating dressed and ready to leave = urgency to leave sin behind, not slowly drift away from it.

Every time you take the sacrament, you're doing the same thing Israel did at Passover — remembering that God delivered you and promising to stay with Him. The bread and water point to Christ, just like the lamb and the blood did.

sections.title: Talk Starters
items: Pharaoh kept making promises during the hard moments and breaking them when things got easy. When are you most likely to forget the spiritual commitments you've made?
What's something in your life right now that you know you should change but keep putting off? What would it take for your heart to actually soften on it?

The Israelites had to DO something to be saved — sacrifice the lamb, put the blood on the door, eat the meal, be ready to leave. What does that teach about our role in the Atonement? Is it enough to just believe?
Why do you think God wants us to remember? What happens to your testimony when you forget what He's done for you?

sections.title: How to Teach It
items: Rock vs. sponge: Bring a rock and a sponge. Pour water on both. Ask which one received the water. Read 1 Nephi 2:16 and Mosiah 3:19. Ask: 'Which one are you being right now?' Simple. Visual. Sticks.

Passover symbols chart: Draw two columns — 'Passover Symbol' and 'What It Means for Us.' Let the boys match them up from Exodus 12. When they see the lamb = Christ on their own, it hits different than you just telling them.
60-second testimony: Share a time YOUR heart was hard and the moment it softened. Be real. These boys can spot fake from a mile away — but they lean in when you're honest about your own struggles.

sections.title: Challenge for the Week
items: Pick ONE thing you've been hardening your heart about — maybe it's how you treat a sibling, something you know you should stop doing, or a prompting you keep ignoring. This week, do the Nephi move: actually pray about it and ask God to soften your heart.

During the sacrament this Sunday, think about the Passover. The bread and water are your lamb's blood on the doorpost. Think about what the Savior is delivering YOU from — and take the ordinance like it matters.
